questions.to_find: The candidate key (minimal superkey) of relation R.

understand.plain: We have a relation with 10 attributes and several functional dependencies. A key is a minimal set of attributes whose closure (applying FDs repeatedly) gives all attributes of R. We test each option to see which one covers all attributes and cannot be reduced further.

keywords.explain: A minimal set of attributes that uniquely determines all other attributes through functional dependencies.
keywords.example: If AB -> all attributes and neither A alone nor B alone can do so, then AB is a candidate key.

keywords.explain: The set of all attributes that can be functionally determined from a given set of attributes using the given FDs.
keywords.example: If A -> B and B -> C, then {A}+ = {A,B,C}.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='220' viewBox='0 0 520 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='38' class='lbl ink'>Closure of {E,F,H}:</text><text x='30' y='62' class='sub ink'>{E,F} -> G  =>  add G</text><text x='30' y='82' class='sub ink'>{F} -> I,J  =>  add I, J</text><text x='30' y='102' class='sub ink'>{E,H} -> K,L  =>  add K, L</text><text x='30' y='122' class='sub ink'>{K} -> M  =>  add M</text><text x='30' y='142' class='sub ink'>{L} -> N  =>  add N</text><rect x='20' y='155' width='470' height='45' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='30' y='183' class='lbl ink'>{E,F,H}+ = {E,F,G,H,I,J,K,L,M,N} = All attributes!</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Step-by-step closure computation of {E,F,H} showing that applying all FDs yields all 10 attributes of R.

given.aim: Find the candidate key of R from the given functional dependencies.

terms.example: All attributes must appear in the closure of the key.
terms.connects: We need to find a minimal subset that determines all 10 attributes.

terms.meaning: E and F together functionally determine G.
terms.example: If two rows agree on E and F, they must agree on G.
terms.connects: E and F together give us G.

terms.meaning: E and H together determine K and L.
terms.example: Combining this with K->M and L->N extends the closure further.
terms.connects: H is needed in the key because without H, K and L cannot be derived.

terms.meaning: F alone determines I and J.
terms.example: Any set containing F can also derive I and J.
terms.connects: F must be in the key to derive I and J.

given.plan: Compute the closure of each candidate option. A valid key must produce all 10 attributes. Then verify minimality by checking that no proper subset of the key can also produce all attributes.

steps.title: Compute closure of {E,F,H}
steps.note: {E,F} alone gives {E,F,G,I,J} — missing H,K,L,M,N. So E and F are not enough; H is required.

steps.title: Verify minimality of {E,F,H}
steps.apply:
{E,F}+ = {E,F,G,I,J} — missing H,K,L,M,N. Not all.
{E,H}+ = {E,H,K,L,M,N} — missing F,G,I,J. Not all.
{F,H}+ = {F,H,I,J} — missing E,G,K,L,M,N. Not all.
No proper subset covers all attributes, so {E,F,H} is minimal, i.e., a candidate key.
steps.note: The answer D. {E} is clearly wrong since {E}+ = {E} only. Option A. {E,F} misses H-dependent attributes. Option C. {E,F,H,I} is a superkey but not minimal (I is redundant).

solution.result: The key for R is {E,F,H} — option B.

questions.formula_note: Attribute closure algorithm: repeatedly apply FDs to expand the set until no new attributes can be added. A candidate key is a minimal superkey.

questions.question:
Given the following two statements:
S1: Every table with two single-valued attributes is in 1NF, 2NF, 3NF and BCNF.
S2: AB -> C, D -> E, E -> C, C -> A is a minimal cover for the set of functional dependencies AB -> C, D -> F, E -> F, F -> A.
Which one of the following is CORRECT?
A. S1 is TRUE and S2 is FALSE.
B. Both S1 and S2 are TRUE.
C. S1 is FALSE and S2 is TRUE.
D. Both S1 and S2 are FALSE.
questions.answer: A. S1 is TRUE and S2 is FALSE.
questions.to_find: Whether S1 (2-attribute table in BCNF) and S2 (given set is minimal cover) are true or false.

understand.plain: S1 asks whether any relation with just two single-valued attributes automatically satisfies all normal forms up to BCNF. S2 asks whether a proposed set of FDs is a minimal (canonical) cover for another set. We need to analyze both statements independently.

keywords.explain: A relation is in BCNF if for every non-trivial FD X->Y, X is a superkey.
keywords.example: In a 2-attribute relation AB, the only possible non-trivial FDs are A->B or B->A, which make A or B a key respectively, satisfying BCNF.

keywords.explain: A minimal cover F_c for a set F has: (1) every RHS is a single attribute, (2) no FD is redundant, (3) no attribute on any LHS is redundant, and (4) F_c is equivalent to F.
keywords.example: If F = {A->BC}, minimal cover splits it to {A->B, A->C}.

understand.visual_alt: Two statements about normalization and minimal covers evaluated for truth value.

given.aim: Determine the truth values of S1 and S2.

terms.meaning: A relation with exactly two single-valued attributes, say A and B.
terms.example: Table T(A,B) — the only non-trivial FDs possible are A->B or B->A.
terms.connects: Any non-trivial FD makes one attribute the key, which is the superkey condition for BCNF.

terms.meaning: AB->C, D->E, E->C, C->A claimed to be equivalent and minimal for AB->C, D->F, E->F, F->A.
terms.example: Must check both equivalence and minimality conditions.
terms.connects: If the two sets have different closures, they are not equivalent, so S2 is false.

terms.meaning: Two FD sets are equivalent iff they produce the same closure for every set of attributes.
terms.example: Check {D}+ under both sets: D->F->A vs D->E->C->A — these yield different sets.
terms.connects: The attribute F is reachable in the original set under D but not in the proposed cover, proving non-equivalence.

given.plan: Verify S1 by reasoning about 2-attribute relations and BCNF definition. Verify S2 by checking closure equivalence between the original FD set and the proposed minimal cover.

steps.title: Evaluate S1: 2-attribute table in BCNF
steps.formula_raw: BCNF: for all X->Y (non-trivial), X is a superkey
steps.apply:
Let the relation be R(A,B). Possible non-trivial FDs:
- No FDs: any single attribute is a key, relation is trivially in BCNF.
- A->B: A is the key; A is a superkey. BCNF holds.
- B->A: B is the key; B is a superkey. BCNF holds.
- A->B and B->A: both are keys. BCNF holds.
In all cases BCNF (and hence 1NF, 2NF, 3NF) is satisfied.
steps.note: 2NF requires no partial dependency on a key, and 3NF requires no transitive dependency. With only 2 attributes, neither can arise.

steps.title: Evaluate S2: Check equivalence of the two FD sets
steps.apply:
Original set F: AB->C, D->F, E->F, F->A.
{D}+ under F: D->F, F->A => {D,F,A}.

Proposed cover Fc: AB->C, D->E, E->C, C->A.
{D}+ under Fc: D->E, E->C, C->A => {D,E,C,A}.

Under F, {D}+ contains F but not E or C (starting from D alone).
Under Fc, {D}+ contains E,C but not F.
The two closures are different (F is in one but not the other), so Fc is NOT equivalent to F.
Hence S2 is FALSE.
steps.note: For a minimal cover to be valid, it must be logically equivalent to the original set — same closure for all attribute subsets. Here the closure of {D} differs, proving non-equivalence.

solution.result: S1 is TRUE, S2 is FALSE — option A.

questions.formula_note: BCNF definition and canonical/minimal cover equivalence check using attribute closures.

questions.to_find: The trivial functional dependency among the given options.

understand.plain: A trivial functional dependency is one where the right-hand side attributes are a subset of the left-hand side attributes. It always holds by definition, not because of any given FD. We look for the option where every attribute on the right also appears on the left.

keywords.explain: An FD X->Y is trivial if Y is a subset of X (Y ⊆ X). It is always true and provides no new information.
keywords.example: {A,B} -> {A} is trivial because A is already in {A,B}.

keywords.explain: The complete set of all functional dependencies that can be derived from F using Armstrong's axioms (reflexivity, augmentation, transitivity).
keywords.example: If A->B and B->C, then A->C is in F+.

understand.visual_alt: Checking each option: option C has RHS {Q,S} which is a subset of LHS {P,Q,S}, making it trivially true.

given.aim: Identify the trivial FD from the options — one where RHS is a subset of LHS.

terms.connects: Checking each option for this subset condition directly identifies the answer.

terms.meaning: S and T are not in {P,R}, so this is non-trivial (a given FD, not trivially true).
terms.example: It holds because it is explicitly given in F.
terms.connects: Not trivial since RHS is not a subset of LHS.

terms.meaning: RHS = {Q,S}, LHS = {P,Q,S}. Clearly Q ∈ LHS and S ∈ LHS, so {Q,S} ⊆ {P,Q,S}.
terms.example: By reflexivity axiom, any superset of {Q,S} determines {Q,S}.
terms.connects: This is the definition of a trivial FD.

given.plan: Check each option: if RHS ⊆ LHS, the FD is trivial. Only option C satisfies this.

steps.title: Test each option for triviality (RHS ⊆ LHS)
steps.formula_raw: Trivial iff Y subset_of X in X -> Y
steps.apply:
A. {P,R} -> {S,T}: S ∉ {P,R} and T ∉ {P,R}. NOT trivial.
B. {P,B} -> {R,T}: R ∉ {P,B} and T ∉ {P,B}. NOT trivial. (Also B is not even in the relation.)
C. {P,Q,S} -> {Q,S}: Q ∈ {P,Q,S} and S ∈ {P,Q,S}. So {Q,S} ⊆ {P,Q,S}. TRIVIAL.
D. {P,Q,R} -> {S,T,U}: S,T,U ∉ {P,Q,R}. NOT trivial (it may be derivable from F but is not trivially true).
steps.note: By Armstrong's reflexivity axiom, X -> Y is always in F+ when Y ⊆ X, regardless of the given FDs.

steps.title: Confirm option C is in F+
steps.formula_raw: Armstrong's Reflexivity: if Y ⊆ X then X -> Y
steps.apply: {Q,S} ⊆ {P,Q,S}, therefore by reflexivity {P,Q,S} -> {Q,S} is in F+ for any FD set F. It is trivially true.
steps.note: Options A and D are non-trivial FDs that happen to be in F+ because of the given FDs. But only C is trivial (true by definition).

solution.result: The trivial functional dependency is {P,Q,S} -> {Q,S} — option C.

questions.formula_note: Armstrong's Reflexivity Axiom: if Y is a subset of X, then X -> Y holds trivially and is in every F+.

questions.question:
Which of the following is NOT a superkey in a relational schema with attributes V, W, X, Y, Z and primary key VY?
A. V, Y, Z
B. V, W, X, Y
C. V, W, Y
D. V, W, X, Z
questions.to_find: The set of attributes that is NOT a superkey given primary key VY.

understand.plain: A superkey is any set of attributes that includes the primary key as a subset. If the primary key is VY, then any set containing both V and Y is a superkey. We need to find the option that does NOT contain both V and Y.

keywords.explain: A set of attributes that can uniquely identify every tuple in a relation. It must contain the primary key (or be the primary key).
keywords.example: If VY is the primary key, then VYZ, VWY, VWXY are all superkeys.

keywords.explain: The minimal superkey chosen to uniquely identify tuples in a relation.
keywords.example: Primary key VY means V and Y together (minimally) determine all other attributes W, X, Z.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='200' viewBox='0 0 420 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='400' height='180' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='40' class='lbl ink'>Primary Key: V, Y</text><text x='30' y='68' class='sub ink'>A. {V,Y,Z}  — contains V and Y ✓ superkey</text><text x='30' y='92' class='sub ink'>B. {V,W,X,Y} — contains V and Y ✓ superkey</text><text x='30' y='116' class='sub ink'>C. {V,W,Y}  — contains V and Y ✓ superkey</text><rect x='20' y='128' width='380' height='42' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='30' y='155' class='lbl ink'>D. {V,W,X,Z} — missing Y ✗ NOT a superkey</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Options A, B, C each contain both V and Y (the primary key), making them superkeys. Option D contains V but not Y, so it cannot uniquely identify tuples.

given.aim: Identify which option does not contain the primary key VY as a subset.

terms.meaning: Both V and Y must be present in any superkey.
terms.example: VY uniquely identifies each row, so any superset also does.
terms.connects: Any option missing V or Y cannot be a superkey.

terms.meaning: Contains V, W, X, Z — but Y is absent.
terms.example: Without Y, the set cannot uniquely identify all tuples since VY is the primary key.
terms.connects: This is the only option missing Y, making it the answer.

terms.meaning: Any superset of a superkey is also a superkey.
terms.example: If VY is a key, then VWY, VYZ, VWXY are all superkeys.
terms.connects: Options A, B, C all contain VY as a subset, so they are automatically superkeys.

given.plan: Check each option: does it contain both V and Y? If yes, it is a superkey. The option missing V or Y is NOT a superkey.

steps.title: Identify the condition for a superkey
steps.formula_raw: Superkey S: primary key PK subset_of S
steps.apply: Primary key = {V, Y}. A set S is a superkey iff {V,Y} ⊆ S, i.e., both V and Y appear in S.
steps.note: Any superset of the primary key uniquely determines all attributes and is therefore a superkey.

steps.title: Check each option
steps.formula_raw: Does the set contain both V and Y?
steps.apply:
A. {V,Y,Z}: contains V? Yes. contains Y? Yes. => Superkey.
B. {V,W,X,Y}: contains V? Yes. contains Y? Yes. => Superkey.
C. {V,W,Y}: contains V? Yes. contains Y? Yes. => Superkey.
D. {V,W,X,Z}: contains V? Yes. contains Y? NO. => NOT a superkey.
steps.note: Option D has V, W, X, Z but is missing Y, which is part of the primary key, so it cannot uniquely identify tuples.

solution.result: VWXZ is NOT a superkey — option D.

questions.formula_note: Superkey definition: a set of attributes S is a superkey if and only if the primary key is a subset of S.
